content.problemStatement:
Chef is cooking his finest dish yet. The recipe calls for a series of NNN steps, where the iii-th step is recommended to be performed at a temperature of AiA_iAi​.

The steps must be performed in order from 111 to NNN.

The further away from the recommended temperature Chef gets, the worse the dish tastes.

Suppose Chef performs the iii-th step at a temperature of BiB_iBi​.

The unsavoriness of the dish is then defined to be

max⁡i=1N∣Ai−Bi∣\max_{i=1}^N |A_i - B_i|i=1maxN​∣Ai​−Bi​∣That is, the unsavoriness is the maximum temperature difference across all the steps.

Note that ∣x∣|x|∣x∣ refers to the absolute value of xxx.

Chef can choose to perform each step at any temperature he wishes - i.e. he can choose the array BBB freely.

However, he doesn't want to change the temperature too often from one step to the next.

A temperature change happens at step iii (where 2≤i≤N2 \leq i \leq N2≤i≤N) if Bi≠Bi−1B_i \neq B_{i-1}Bi​=Bi−1​.

For example, if B=[1,1,2,2,6,3]B = [1, 1, 2, 2, 6, 3]B=[1,1,2,2,6,3], there are three temperature changes: at steps 3,5,3, 5, 3,5, and 666.

You are the given the array AAA denoting recommended temperatures.

If Chef can choose the array BBB freely, and change temperature **at most** KKK times from one step to the next, what's the **minimum** possible unsavoriness of the dish?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK — the number of steps in the recipe and the maximum allowed number of temperature changes, respectively.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, the recommended  temperature of the NNN steps.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line one integer: the minimum possible unsavoriness.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: K=0K = 0K=0, so there cannot be any temperature changes.
It's optimal for Chef to choose B=[2,2,2]B = [2, 2, 2]B=[2,2,2].
This gives:

∣A1−B1∣=∣1−2∣=1|A_1 - B_1| = |1 - 2| = 1∣A1​−B1​∣=∣1−2∣=1
∣A2−B2∣=∣3−2∣=1|A_2 - B_2| = |3 - 2| = 1∣A2​−B2​∣=∣3−2∣=1
∣A3−B3∣=∣3−2∣=1|A_3 - B_3| = |3 - 2| = 1∣A3​−B3​∣=∣3−2∣=1

All differences are 111, so the unsavoriness - the maximum difference - is 111. This is optimal.
Test case 222: Same as the previous case, but K=1K = 1K=1 so one temperature change is allowed.
Chef can choose B=[1,3,3]B = [1, 3, 3]B=[1,3,3], the same as AAA, which has only one temperature change. The unsavoriness is 000 since Ai=BiA_i = B_iAi​=Bi​ for all iii.
Test case 333: One optimal choice of BBB is B=[27,44,44,27]B = [27, 44, 44, 27]B=[27,44,44,27]. There are two temperature changes (at steps 222 and 444), which is allowed because K=2K = 2K=2.
It can be verified that ∣A2−B2∣=4|A_2 - B_2| = 4∣A2​−B2​∣=4 is the maximum difference. 444 is optimal.
Test case 444: One optimal choice of BBB is B=[101,101,101,101,90]B = [101, 101, 101, 101, 90]B=[101,101,101,101,90], which has one temperature change.
